Architecture Type: Indo-Islamic sepulchral architecture
UNESCO Criteria: (i) to represent a masterpiece of human creative genius;
Local Stone Name: Makrana Marble (Sang-e-Marmar / Pure White Calcite Marble)
Lithology: Metamorphic (Granoblastic Calcitic Marble with ~98–100% Calcite); Secondary stone is Sedimentary Quartzarenite Sandstone
Formation: Ajmer Formation, Kumbhalgarh Group, Delhi Supergroup
Texture: Fine to medium-grained, crystalline granoblastic, interlocking texture
Minerals: Calcite (>98%), with minor quartz, muscovite, and silicate impurities in grey streaks
Quarry: Makrana (Nagaur District, Rajasthan) for marble; Tantpur/Dholpur for red sandstone
Local vs Imported: Imported (Transported from Nagaur, Rajasthan to Agra, Uttar Pradesh)
Transport Distance: ~300 km to 350 km (from Makrana, Rajasthan to Agra)
Structural Use: Main tomb structure, minarets, platforms, domes, and wall veneering.
Decorative Use: Exquisite octagonal marble lattice screens, pietra dura stone intarsia inlay (using precious/semi-precious stones), floral relief motifs, and Arabic calligraphic inscriptions.
Masonry Technique: Brick-in-lime mortar core veneered with dressed stone slices.
Weathering: Atmospheric pollution-induced discolouration; micro-cracking and bio-deterioration
Restoration: Traditional clay pack treatments (Fuller's earth / multani mitti) for cleaning marble yellowing; air quality monitoring; banning coal/coke usage in the 10,400 $\text{km}^2$ Taj Trapezium Zone (TTZ)
Other references:

Site Name: Historical Centre of the City of Arequipa
Architecture Type: Colonial, Andean Baroque (Mestizo Baroque), Rococo, Neoclassical, Vernacular (Casonas), Historicist
Civilization: Spanish Colonial, European integrated with Native / Indigenous (Pre-Hispanic / Inca influence)
Rock Class_Ext: Bustamante, R., Vazquez, P., & Prendes, N. (2021). Properties of the Ignimbrites in the Architecture of the Historical Center of Arequipa, Peru. Applied Sciences, 11(22), 10571. https://doi.org/10.3390/app112210571
Lithology: Dacitic to Rhyolitic Non-welded / Indurated Ignimbrite (Vitric Tuff / Pyroclastic Flow Deposit)
Lithology_Ext: Bustamante, R., Vazquez, P., & Prendes, N. (2021). Properties of the Ignimbrites in the Architecture of the Historical Center of Arequipa, Peru. Applied Sciences, 11(22), 10571. https://doi.org/10.3390/app112210571
Quarry: Canteras de Añashuayco (Añashuayco Ravine / Paccha Quarry), Airport Quarry
Local vs Imported: Local (Extracted from nearby volcanic quarries surrounding the city)
Structural Use: Robust load-bearing walls, foundations, semi-circular arches, porticos, thick vaults, pillars, bridges (Puente Real / Puente Grau)
Decorative Use: Intricate Baroque facade relief carvings, carved corbels, protruding pediments, cornices, decorative doorways, window flanking pillars
Masonry Technique: Ashlar masonry (Sillar blocks, voussoirs, paving tiles cut to sizes such as 40x40x20 cubic cm), intricate hand carving by native masons
Weathering: Damage due to seismic activity (earthquakes), low-intensity volcanic activity, ENSO/flooding, high atmospheric pollution from vehicle emissions, and erosion
Restoration: Rebuilding and adaptive reuse following major earthquakes; ~20% of declared casonas/monuments are fully restored
Other references: Advisory Body Evaluation (ICOMOS 2000); Peruvian National Law Nº 28296; Municipal Ordinance Nº 13-99; Peer-reviewed geological and architectural studies on Arequipa ignimbrites

Architecture Type: a complex fusion of Swahili, Arab, Indian, and European influences

Architecture Type: Imperial Roman Classical Architecture
Construction Period: 2nd century A.D. (117 – 138 A.D., reign of Emperor Hadrian)
UNESCO Criteria: (i) to represent a masterpiece of human creative genius; | (ii) to exhibit an important interchange of human values, over a span of time or within a cultural area of the world, on developments in architecture or technology, monumental arts, town-planning or landscape design; | (iii) to bear a unique or at least exceptional testimony to a cultural tradition or to a civilization which is living or which has disappeared;
Mentioned Major Stone(s): Cipollino Marble (Marmor Carystium), Granite, Marble, Travertine (Lapis Tiburtinus), Tufa
Secondary Stone: Pavonazzetto, Porfido Rosso (Red Porphyry), Proconnesian Marble
Colour: Greenish-white with dark green bands (Cipollino); Grey/Pink/Red (Granite); Light beige to cream (Travertine)
Quarry: Euboea/Carystos quarries (Cipollino); Aswan quarries (Egyptian Granite); Tivoli / Barco quarries (Travertine & Tufa)
Local vs Imported: Both: Travertine and Tufa are Local; Cipollino Marble and Egyptian Granite are Imported
Structural Use: Structural piers, load-bearing walls, foundations, and vaults (Tufa and Travertine); load-bearing columns (Travertine & Granite)
Decorative Use: Peristyle column shafts (Cipollino marble and Egyptian granite), floor pavements (opus sectile), wall revetments, porticos, and decorative statues
Masonry Technique: Opus reticulatum, opus mixtum, opus testaceum (brick-faced concrete core), and opus quadratum (travertine ashlar block masonry)
Weathering: Atmospheric weathering, carbonate dissolution, biological growth, and material degradation from past exposure/neglect
Restoration: Partial anastylosis, continuous consolidation, and structural preservation carried out under the Restoration Charter and the Italian Codice dei beni culturali e del Paesaggio
Other references:
• ICOMOS Advisory Body Evaluation - Villa Adriana (Tivoli)


• Scientific studies on Roman building stone provenance published via the Italian Journal of Geosciences.

Architecture Type: Fortified city, Water Reservoirs, and Cemetery
Construction Period: ca. 3000–1500 BCE (Bronze Age / Early, Mature, and Late Harappan phases)
UNESCO Criteria: (iii) to bear a unique or at least exceptional testimony to a cultural tradition or to a civilization which is living or which has disappeared; | (iv) to be an outstanding example of a type of building, architectural or technological ensemble or landscape which illustrates (a) significant stage(s) in human history;
Lithology: Calcareous Sandstone, Calcareous Mudstone, Fossiliferous Limestone
Colour: Yellowish-brown, reddish-brown, buff sandstone, white/banded limestone
Quarry: Ancient stone quarry sites on Khadir Island (located in the buffer zone)
Local vs Imported:
Local: Architectural Sandstone & Limestone from Khadir Island.

Imported/Traded: Raw minerals, metals, and semi-precious stones via regional trade.

Architecture Type: Victorian Gothic Revival blended with traditional Indian architecture
UNESCO Criteria: (ii) to exhibit an important interchange of human values, over a span of time or within a cultural area of the world, on developments in architecture or technology, monumental arts, town-planning or landscape design; | (iv) to be an outstanding example of a type of building, architectural or technological ensemble or landscape which illustrates (a) significant stage(s) in human history;
Mentioned Major Stone(s): Yellow Malad Stone, Italian Marble, Polished Granite, White Limestone
Secondary Stone: Italian Marble, Polished Granite, White Limestone
Lithology: Trachytic/Basaltic tuff (Malad yellow stone), crystalline marble, fine-grained limestone
Local vs Imported: Mixed (Local yellow Malad stone; Imported Italian marble)
Transport Distance: Local (<25 km) for Malad stone; International long-distance sea route for Italian marble
Structural Use: Octagonal ribbed masonry dome, drum, turrets, pointed arches, main entrance gates, columns, and exterior walls
Decorative Use: 3D-stone carvings of local flora and fauna, gargoyles, allegorical grotesques, arched tympana, portrait roundels, relief busts, decorated rose windows, lion/tiger gate pillars
Masonry Technique: Ashlar stone masonry, octagonal ribbed dome masonry, bas-relief and openwork stone carving
Weathering: Atmospheric degradation from air pollution/traffic emissions and chemical weathering from coastal saline air
Restoration: Phase II conservation works ongoing under the long-term management plan by the Architectural Conservation Cell (ACC, initiated 1997)
Other references:
ICOMOS Advisory Body Evaluation,

Maitri Dore (2014)

Architecture Type: Palatine Chapel / Cathedral (Carolingian octagonal core with Gothic choir and additions)

Local vs Imported: Local (immediate vicinity) to Long-distance (>1,000 km from Rome and Ravenna)

Decorative Use: Antique interior marble columns/facade lining, Carolingian stone throne, Proserpine relief sarcophagus

Site Name: Mahabodhi Temple Complex at Bodh Gaya
Architecture Type: Classical style of Indian temple architecture (featuring a 50m curvilinear shikhara/tower, chaitya niches, stupas, and shrines)
Civilization: Mauryan Period & Late Gupta Period (Indian Buddhist)
Colour: Black (for footprint stone / Padas); NA for sandstone colour
Structural Use:
Sandstone: Pillars of the balustrade, Vajrasana (Diamond Throne) platform.


Black Stone: Footprint slab (Padas).


(Bricks form the main body, walls, and tower)
Decorative Use: Sculpted human faces, animals, decorative honeysuckle and geese motifs, chaitya niches, carved Buddha footprints
Masonry Technique: Late Gupta period brick masonry; stone carving for balustrades and relics
Restoration: Suffered long abandonment (13th–18th century A.D.); extensively restored in the 19th century A.D. and further conserved in the late 20th century under guidance of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI)
Other references:
ICOMOS Advisory Body Evaluation;
 Bodhgaya Temple Management Committee (BTMC), Archaeological Survey of India (ASI)

Transport Distance: Minimal to none (In-situ carving or immediate local quarrying)
Decorative Use: Bas-relief panels, monolithic animal sculptures, carved pillars, and wall motifs
Masonry Technique: Direct rock-cut carving (monolithic) and dressed ashlar masonry blocks

Architecture Type: Dravidian Architecture (Temples, Gopurams, Mandapas) and Indo-Islamic Architecture (Secular buildings like Queen's Bath and Elephant Stables).
Construction Period: 14th to 16th Century CE (Vijayanagara Empire, 1336–1565 CE; building activity spanning over 200 years).
Civilization: Vijayanagara Empire (The last great Hindu Kingdom of South India).
Local vs Imported: Local ("constructed from local granite", "utilizing locally available material")
Structural Use: Fortification walls, temple complexes, pillared halls (Mandapas), shrines, entrance towers (Gopurams), ceremonial pavilions, heavy roof slabs, stone chariot (granite ratha), stepped tanks (Pushkarani), bridges, and water channels.
Decorative Use: Intricately carved stone chariot (Vittala Temple), ornate decorated pillars, relief sculptures, carved entrance gopurams, and sculpted lintels/capitals.
Masonry Technique: Post-and-lintel system, lantern-roofed construction, dry-stone masonry with paper joints (rubble core for fortification walls), brick-and-lime mortar superstructures (for Gopuras), and heavy granite roof slabs sealed with lime mortar waterproofing.
Restoration: Ongoing conservation and preservation by the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) and Department of Archaeology and Museums (DAM), guided by the Integrated Management Plan (IMP) and Hampi World Heritage Area Management Authority (HWHAMA).
Other references: Official ICOMOS Evaluation & UNESCO Dossier Documents

Site Name: Cathedral, Torre Civica and Piazza Grande, Modena
Architecture Type: Cathedral, Bell Tower ("Ghirlandina"), Civic Square
UNESCO Criteria: (i) to represent a masterpiece of human creative genius; | (ii) to exhibit an important interchange of human values, over a span of time or within a cultural area of the world, on developments in architecture or technology, monumental arts, town-planning or landscape design; | (iii) to bear a unique or at least exceptional testimony to a cultural tradition or to a civilization which is living or which has disappeared; | (iv) to be an outstanding example of a type of building, architectural or technological ensemble or landscape which illustrates (a) significant stage(s) in human history;
Quarry: Reused ancient Roman architectural remains (Mutina ruins)
Local vs Imported: Local reuse (spolia from ancient Roman sites in Modena)
Decorative Use: Relief sculptures (Wiligelmo), exterior wall reliefs, internal capitals, metopes
Masonry Technique: Ashlar stone cladding over brick/masonry core; Roman stone reuse
Weathering: Stone deterioration, environmental pollution, micro-cracks from seismic shaking
Replacement Stone: Replacement of eight original metopes with copies (originals moved to museum)
Restoration: Post-WWII conservative restoration, 1950s crypt restoration, 1970s–1980s stone deterioration repairs, post-1996 and 2012 seismic interventions
Other references: UNESCO Dossier & ICOMOS Document Links

Architecture Type: Dry-stone monumental masonry architecture (Hill Ruins, Great Enclosure, Valley Ruins, Conical Tower, Enclosure Walls)
Secondary Stone: Soapstone / Steatite (used for upright posts and carved Zimbabwe Birds); Granitic sand and clay mixture (Daga)
Local Stone Name: Daga (traditional mixture of clay and granitic sand used for bricks, huts, and floors)
Quarry: Local granite hill outcrops and naturally exfoliated granite spalls surrounding the Great Zimbabwe site
Local vs Imported: Local (Granite obtained directly at the site; Steatite sourced regionally within Zimbabwe)
Structural Use: Enclosure perimeter walls, Conical Tower, passageways, foundations, and lintels
Decorative Use: Chevron pattern, chequered wall decorations, carved upright ritual posts, and soapstone bird figurines
Masonry Technique: Dry stone masonry without mortar, laid in regular horizontal courses using split/dressed granite blocks
Weathering: Temperature fluctuations, soil moisture variations, and invasive vegetation encroachment (Lantana camara)
Restoration: Dry-stone wall collapse monitoring, vegetation control, and structural stabilization under the National Museums and Monuments of Zimbabwe (NMMZ)
Other references: ICOMOS Advisory Body Evaluation, ICCROM Preservation Study, MDPI Heritage & Sustainability Article

Architecture Type: Garden-Tomb / Dynastic Mausoleum (Mughal / Indo-Islamic Architecture)

Site Name: Millenary Benedictine Abbey of Pannonhalma and its Natural Environment
Architecture Type: Monastic complex (Abbey, Basilica/Church, Cloister, Crypt, Refectory, Chapel, Millennium Monument, Library)
Construction Period: Monastic complex (Abbey, Basilica/Church, Cloister, Crypt, Refectory, Chapel, Millennium Monument, Library)
Civilization: Medieval & Early Modern European / Hungarian Christian (Benedictine Monasticism)
Local vs Imported: Regional / Domestic (Carpathian Basin)

Decorative Use: Porta Speciosa facing, decorative small columns, sculptured cloister stones, portico columns (Ionic), tympanum relief

Architecture Type: Monastic complex; Raška School of eastern medieval church architecture blending Romanesque (exterior) and Byzantine (interior/frescoes) styles. Single-nave domed churches.
Construction Period: Late 12th century (established ~1183–1196 by Stefan Nemanja); King’s Church added in 1314
Civilization: Medieval Serbian State / Serbian Orthodox Church (Nemanjić Dynasty).
Local Stone Name_Ext: Debljović Ristić, N., Šekularac, N., Mijović, D., & Ivanović Šekularac, J. (2019). Studenica Marble: Significance, Use, Conservation. Sustainability, 11(14), 3916. https://doi.org/10.3390/su11143916
Quarry: Quarries located in the immediate protected surrounding zone near the monastery (along the Studenica/Radočelo mountain area)
Local vs Imported: Local
Transport Distance: Short / Local transport (within a few kilometers of the complex)
Structural Use: Load-bearing exterior walls, portals, window openings (biforas/triforas), and structural masonry of the Church of the Virgin
Decorative Use: Sculpted relief portals, decorative capitals, cornices, and window frames

Site Name: Sudanese style mosques in northern Côte d’Ivoire
Architecture Type: Sudanese-style mosque architecture
Construction Period: Originated 12th–14th centuries (Djenné); spread 15th century; surviving mosques built mainly between the 17th and 19th centuries
Civilization: Islamic / Mandinka (Mande) and local indigenous Gur communities
Lithology: Clay, silt, organic temper, and localized lateritic earth
Colour: Earth tones (brownish-yellow, tan, reddish-brown earthen plaster)
Texture: Smooth to coarse earthen plaster finish
Local vs Imported: Local
Structural Use: Load-bearing walls, vertical buttresses, massive pilasters, truncated pyramid minarets, qibla towers, roof support
Decorative Use: Projecting wooden poles (torons), pottery or ostrich egg finials topping buttresses and minarets, mitres crowning roof
Masonry Technique: Cob (bousillage) and adobe (earthen brick) masonry layered with earthen plaster, reinforced with timber scaffolding (torons)
Weathering: Erosion and degradation caused by humid climatic conditions, intense rain, urbanization, and population growth
Replacement Stone: Earth, timber, and traditional clay plasters (Reversible modern materials used in recent interventions need replacement with traditional adobe/cob)
Restoration: Regular community-led maintenance, re-plastering by traditional masons, local grassroots management committees
Other references: Advisory Body Evaluation (ICOMOS), Decree No. 2020-121, Law No. 87-806, Law No. 98-750

Site Name: Castles of Augustusburg and Falkenlust at Brühl
Architecture Type: Palatial ensemble comprising a 18th-century Rococo residence (Augustusburg Palace), a hunting lodge (Falkenlust), adjoining orangeries, and French-style gardens.

Site Name: Collegiate Church, Castle and Old Town of Quedlinburg
Architecture Type: Romanesque basilica (Collegiate Church of St. Servatius), Renaissance/Baroque castle residence, and timber-framed urban vernacular buildings (Fachwerk).
Construction Period: Founded 10th century (922–936 AD); current Romanesque church built 1070–1129 AD; timber-framed houses span the 16th–17th centuries.
Civilization: Ottonian Empire / Holy Roman Empire / Medieval German State.

Architecture Type: Palace and water garden (Grottoes, fountains, ornamental basins)
Construction Period: 16th Century (Laid out in 1550s; works began around 1560)
Secondary Stone: Volcanic Tuff / Stucco (Jet noted in automated classification)
Structural Use: Main body of the palace, stair ramps, retaining walls, terrace foundations
Decorative Use: Fountains (e.g., Fontana del Bicchierone), grottoes, balustrades, ornamental basins
Weathering: Rapid deterioration of decorations/fountains due to micro-climatic conditions and continuous water contact
Restoration: Methodical mural and fountain restorations per the Venice Charter
Other references:
- UNESCO Dossier & OUV Statement


- ISPRA Travertines of Central Italy


- Centroni, A. (2008). Villa d'Este a Tivoli: quattro secoli di storia e restauri

Site Name: Fort and Shalamar Gardens in Lahore
Architecture Type: Fortified Palace & Water Garden Enclosure Complex
Construction Period: 16th – 17th Century (Akbar 1542–1605 through Shah Jahan 1627–1658; Shalamar Gardens completed 1641–1642)
Secondary Stone: Semi-precious stones (pietra dura / inlay), Glass mosaics, Gilt
Colour: Red/Terracotta (Sandstone); Pure White, Milky White, Grey/Pink Streaked (Marble)
Structural Use: Baked brick masonry core with red sandstone courses, enclosing walls, gateways, bastions, and supporting pillars/pavilions
Decorative Use: Luxurious marble palaces, screens, inlay of semi-precious stones (pietra dura), glass mosaics (Shish Mahal), gilt, and zoomorphic corbels
Masonry Technique: Ashlar stone cladding over baked brick, carved stone lattice (jaali) screens, corbelled brackets, and stone inlay/pietra dura
Weathering: Deterioration of hydraulic systems, moisture-induced micro-cracking, thermo-hygric expansion/bowing in marble, atmospheric pollution decay
Replacement Stone: Traditional lime mortar and matching red sandstone/marble matching historical composition during controlled restoration
Restoration: Traditional lime mortar and matching red sandstone/marble matching historical composition during controlled restoration
Other references:
1. Makrana Marble: A Popular Heritage Stone Resource
2. The Art of Pietra-dura during the 17th Century Mughal Era
3. Conservation Challenges and Emerging Trends in Pakistan

Architecture Type: Multifunctional structure of the khanaqa type (combines a mausoleum and a mosque)
Civilization: Timurid Empire / Central Asian Islamic Civilization
Minerals: Gypsum, Carbonates, nitrates, sulfates, chlorides (in salt efflorescence)
Local vs Imported: Local clay for fired bricks; Persian master craft influence for decorative techniques
Structural Use: Vaults, domes (conic-spherical dome), walls, arches, drums
Decorative Use: Alabaster stalactites (muqarnas) in dome intrados, glazed tile facing, inscriptions (Kufic and Suls)
Masonry Technique: Fired brick masonry with plaster carving and glazed tile revetments
Weathering: Deterioration from rising damp, salt efflorescence (nitrates, chlorides, sulfates, carbonates), and high water table
Restoration: Rebuilding of northern citadel wall in 1970s; ongoing conservation projects under "Kazrestavratziya"
Other references:
1: Sainova, G. А., Аubakirov, N. P., Аkbasova, А. D., Yuldashbek, D. H., & Sunakbaeva, D. K. (2023). The Influence of Salt Deposits on the Preservation of the Historical and Architectural Monument of the Mausoleum of Khoja Ahmed Yasawi.

2: Valentinovna, M. A. Islamic architecture of Iran in the era of Timur and Timurids: specificity of the artistic image.

Country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
Architecture Type: Megalithic stone circles, henges, barrows, avenues, and earthen earthworks/mounds.
Construction Period: Circa 3700 BC – 1600 BC (Neolithic to Early Bronze Age); focal stone circle built ~2500 BC.
Civilization: Prehistoric Britain (Neolithic and Bronze Age societies).
Mentioned Major Stone(s): Wiltshire Sarsen Sandstone and Bluestone (dolerite, rhyolite, tuffs)
Secondary Stone: Chalk (used in earthworks/mounds like Silbury Hill), Altar Stone (Metasandstone), Quartz veins, packing stones
Local vs Imported:
Sarsens: Local/Regional (~25 km).
Bluestones: Imported (~200–240 km).
Local vs Imported_Ext: Nash, D. J., Ciborowski, T. J. R., Ullyott, J. S., Pearson, M. P., Darvill, T., Greaney, S., Maniatis, G., & Whitaker, K. A. (2020). Origins of the sarsen megaliths at Stonehenge. Science Advances, 6(31), eabc0133. https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.abc0133
Transport Distance: ~25 km for Sarsens; up to 240 km for Bluestones.
Structural Use: Standing megalithic uprights, horizontal lintels, central trilithons, inner/outer concentric circles, horseshoe arrangements, packing stones.
Decorative Use: Unique surface shaping/dressing of megalithic stones; intentional arrangement of bluestone contrast.
Masonry Technique: Mortise-and-tenon jointing, tongue-and-groove lintel capping, dressed/shaped megaliths.
Weathering: Weathering from surface erosion, natural environmental exposure, animal burrowing, and historic visitor traffic.
Restoration: Early to mid-20th-century interventions involving excavation, re-erection of fallen or buried stones to their original positions, and modern site consolidation.
Other references:
1. Nash et al., 2020 - Science Advances


2. Parker Pearson et al., 2015 - Antiquity


3. Whitaker, 2019 - Oxford Journal of Archaeology


4. Lott, 2013 - Quarterly Journal of Engineering Geology and Hydrogeology

Architecture Type: Earthquake Baroque / Fortress Baroque (Peripheral Baroque)
Civilization: Spanish Colonial Philippines (fusion of Spanish/European, Chinese, and native Philippine traditions)
Mentioned Major Stone(s): Tuff (e.g., volcanic tuff/adobe), Coralline / Coralline Limestone, Brick
Local Stone Name: Adobe stone (volcanic tuff in Manila/Luzon); Coral stone / De capaza
Lithology: Volcanic Tuff, Biogenic Coralline Limestone, Clay Brick
Quarry: Local coastal coral reefs and nearby river quarries (e.g., Meycauayan/Guadalupe tuff quarries)
Quarry Country: Local coastal coral reefs and nearby river quarries (e.g., Meycauayan/Guadalupe tuff quarries)
Local vs Imported: Local
Structural Use: Load-bearing exterior walls, massive buttresses (contrafuertes), bell towers, stone barrel vaults
Decorative Use: High Baroque retablos (altars), volutes, pediment reliefs, carved facades, trompe-l'œil ceiling paintings
Masonry Technique: Thick unreinforced block masonry consolidated with lime mortar
Weathering: Environmental degradation and surface erosion due to time, tropical humidity, and salt air
Restoration: Careful governmental and tri-partite restoration using original materials to preserve integrity
Other references: ICOMOS Advisory Body Evaluation; Presidential Decrees 260 & 375; RA 10066 (National Heritage Law)

Site Name: Historic Centre of the City of Pienza
Architecture Type: Italian Renaissance, Late Gothic (interior of Cathedral)
Construction Period: Late 15th Century (began in 1459 under Pope Pius II)
Structural Use: Load-bearing walls, columns, bell tower foundation, framing
Decorative Use: Herringbone square paving, facade details, capitals, mouldings
Masonry Technique: Ashlar masonry, carved stone framing, herringbone paving
Restoration: Programs ongoing since early 20th century in accordance with Restoration Charters
